coredata.dc:title: Doping effect and fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-doped graphene quantum dots in the detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Y.
Chen, A.Y.
Xie, X.F.
Wang, X.Y.
Wang, D.
Wang, P.
Li, H.J.
Yang, J.H.
Li, Y.
coredata.dc:description: Element doping is recognized as a powerful way to modify surface defect structure and further enhance the fluorescence performance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). N-doped, S-doped and S, N co-doped GQDs were synthesized to explore the influence of element doping on fluorescence sensing of dopamine (DA) biomolecules. Two interesting works are found, one is that the N-doped GQDs with urea as N source are more effective than the S-doped and S,N co-doped GQDs, characterized by the higher quantum yield (QY) up to 78% and sensitive fluorescence quenching performance to DA. The other is that the N-doped GQDs with ethylenediamine as N source have the highest QY up to 95%, however, exhibits no quenching performance to DA. This abnormal observation is discussed based on the microstructure analysis. Under the optimal reaction condition, the N-doped GQDs exhibit a dual linear relationship of quenching intensity with DA concentration in the range of 10–3000 nM and 3000–7000 nM with detection limits of 3.3 and 611 nM, respectively. The quenching mechanism of N-doped GQDs toward DA is explored from the view of N chemical states, biomolecule structure of DA homologues and redox reaction of DA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Doping effect
Fluorescent sensing
Dopamine
Sensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-doped, S-doped and S,N co-doped GQDs
Optical performances of the doped GQDs
Detection sensitivity of the N-GQDs to DA
Effect of the fine structures on the quenching performance
Quenching mechanism of N-GQDs to DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical performances of the as-prepared GQDs. (a) UV–vis spectra; (b) PL spectra; (c) quantum yield; (d) fluorescence response curves of doped GQDs to DA. DA: 20 μM.

Optical performances of the N-GQDs. (a) UV–vis spectra and PL spectra; (b) Raman spectra; (c) Quantum yield; (d) Fluorescence response curves of N-GQDs to DA. DA: 20 μM. The inset in (a) is the PL spectra of N-GQD-8 under different excitation wavelengths, and that in (b) is the fitting curves of N-GQD-8.

Microstructure of the N-GQD-8. (a) TEM image; (b) HRTEM image; (c) Illustration diagram of GQD; (d) AFM image. The inset in (a) shows the size distribution obtained from TEM images, and that in (b) is the magnification after inverse Fourier transform. The inset in (d is the height profile along the indicated line AB.

Fluorescence quenching performance of N-GQD-8 toward DA. (a) PL emission spectra of N-GQD-8 containing DA with different concentrations; (b) Concentration-dependent fluorescence response; (c) Selectivity of N-GQD-8; (d) Interference experiments, where the concentration of DA and interfering biomolecules are both 10 μM in 0.1 M PBS.
XPS spectra of the GQD, N-GQD-8 and N-GQD-EDA-4. (a) Survey spectra; (b) and (c) N 1s of N-GQD-8 and N-GQD-EDA-4, respectively; (d) N content of the different N-GQDs.

Effect of the DA homologues on the fluorescence quenching performance of N-GQD-8. (a) Structure diagram of N-GQD and DA homologues; (b) Fluorescence response curves of N-GQD-8 after addition of different biomolecules (2 μM) in 0.1 M PBS; (c) PL spectra of N-GQD-8 after addition of different biomolecules (2 μM) in 0.1 M PBS; The inset gives the ratio of fluorescence intensity before and after adding quenchers. (d) PL spectra of N-GQD/DA-quinone and N-GQD/o-benzoquinone after oxidation of N-GQD/DA and N-GQD/catechol by H2O2. H2O2:10 μM, DA: 2 μM, catechol: 2 μM. The inset shows the ratio of fluorescence intensity before and after adding quenchers.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-GQD toward DA.
The comparisons of different fluorometric sensors for DA.
Determination results of DA content in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Doping effect and fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-doped graphene quantum dots in the detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Innovation Institution for Materials, Shanghai 200444, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science & Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Element doping is recognized as a powerful way to modify surface defect structure and further enhance the fluorescence performance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). N-doped, S-doped and S, N co-doped GQDs were synthesized to explore the influence of element doping on fluorescence sensing of dopamine (DA) biomolecules. Two interesting works are found, one is that the N-doped GQDs with urea as N source are more effective than the S-doped and S,N co-doped GQDs, characterized by the higher quantum yield (QY) up to 78% and sensitive fluorescence quenching performance to DA. The other is that the N-doped GQDs with ethylenediamine as N source have the highest QY up to 95%, however, exhibits no quenching performance to DA. This abnormal observation is discussed based on the microstructure analysis. Under the optimal reaction condition, the N-doped GQDs exhibit a dual linear relationship of quenching intensity with DA concentration in the range of 10–3000 nM and 3000–7000 nM with detection limits of 3.3 and 611 nM, respectively. The quenching mechanism of N-doped GQDs toward DA is explored from the view of N chemical states, biomolecule structure of DA homologues and redox reaction of DA.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new class of photoluminescent nanomaterials, have received intensive attention in sensing applications due to their aqueous dispersibility, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and high photostability [1,2]. As desirable fluorescent probes, GQDs have presented special abilities for sensing various targets, such as metal ions, small organic molecules, and large biochemical molecules [3–7]. However, the practical implementation of GQD as sensing probe is still in its initial stage because of the low quantum yield (QY) [8–10]. Heteroatom doping of GQD is an effective way to tune the fluorescence property by inducing new functional groups or producing interactions among carbon atoms with doping atoms. Many researchers have reported special properties of heteroatom doping, especially N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) and S-doped GQDs (S-GQDs) [3,4,11–13]. For example, Yang et al. [13] successfully prepared N-GQDs and S-GQDs by one-step hydrothermal method, where the QY increased from 6% (GQDs) to 39% (N-GQDs) and 24% (S-GQDs). These doped GQDs have high sensitivity for the fluorescence detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+, respectively, which can be attributed to the electron or energy transfer process via the strong coordination bonds between metal ions and doped elements. Although the photoluminescence of GQDs shows high dependency on the doping effect, the influence of fine structures, such as chemical groups, on the sensing property still requires more studies, especially in the sensing systems with complicated biological molecules.

Dopamine (DA) is an indispensable catecholamine neurotransmitter that mainly exists in central nervous system of mammals, and plays an important role in controlling the central nervous system-related diseases [14,15], such as Parkinson's disease [16,17], schizophrenia and depression [18]. Accurate analysis of DA could greatly benefit for the clinical diagnosis of special neurological diseases. Recently, fluorescent method has attracted more interest because of its high sensitivity, good reliability, operational simplicity, real-time detection, and low cost [19–21]. Therefore, an effective probe for label-free DA fluorescence detection is developed based on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs. For instance, Zhao et al. [19] used pyrogenic citric acid (CA) to synthesize GQDs for the detection of DA with a linear range of 0–50 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 90 nM. They thought the formation of dopamine-aerosols by the oxidation of DA in aerobic environments resulted in fluorescence quenching through electron transfer. Chen and co-workers [20] proposed a visual fluorescence sensor for the detection of DA with a linearity range of 0–200 μM and a LOD of 70 nM. The quenching mechanism was ascribed to the electron transfer from the photo-excited N-GQDs to dopamine-quinine, which was produced via the oxidization of DA by ambient O2 in an alkaline solution. Weng et al. [22] developed a fluorescence quenching mechanism for sensing DA, which utilized the self-polymerization of DA to form a poly-dopamine (pDA) thin film on the surface of GQDs. Thus, the signal-off quenching mechanisms are very complicated, involving electron transfer from photo-excited GQDs to dopamine-quinine, fluorescence resonance energy transfer by assembling a thin layer [23], or other interactions occurring at the GQDs surface. Due to the low concentration of DA in the complicated biological system, a new probe for sensing DA with high sensitivity and anti-interference ability is still a challenging research topic. Furthermore, the fundamental mechanism of GQDs for the DA detection remains unclear, especially after the element doping.

In this work, we propose a simple, selective, and sensitive signal-off fluorescence strategy for DA detection by doped GQDs. In order to enhance the QY, N (strong electronegativity) and S (weak electronegativity) are selected as the doping elements to prepare the doped GQDs by one-step hydrothermal method. The N-doped GQDs with urea (Ur) as precursor exhibit a dual linear relationship of quenching intensity with DA concentration. The doping effect and QY on the sensing sensitivity of GQDs to DA are discussed, and detailed quenching mechanism of N-GQDs toward DA are systematically investigated from the view of surface chemical states of fluorescer and molecule structures of the quencher.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), dipotassium phosphate (K2HPO3), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO3), sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S·9H2O), Ur, thiourea (Us), CA, glucose (Gs), glycine (Gly), L-cysteine (L-Cys), ascorbic acid (AA) and β-phenethylamine (β-PEA) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA), anhydrous ethylenediamine (EDA) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sigma Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate and rhodamine-6G (R6G) were purchased from Shanghai Lanji Technology Development Co., Ltd. Catechol, β-phenethylamine and tyramine were purchased from Hefei BASF Biological Technology Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped, S-doped and S,N co-doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three types of doped GQDs were prepared, that is the N-GQDs, S-GQDs, and S,N co-doped GQDs (S,N-GQDs). Among the N-GQDs, two precursors of Ur and EDA were used as the N source. 1.68 g CA and 1.44 g Ur were dissolved into 40 mL water and then transferred into a 50 mL stainless autoclave. The mixed solution was reacted at 180 °C for 4, 8 and 16 h, and the obtained specimens were denoted as N-GQD-4, N-GQD-8 and N-GQD-16, respectively. When selecting EDA as the N source, the hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 4 h by using 1.68 g CA and 1.6 mL EDA as the starting materials. The final product was referred to as N-GQD-EDA-4. For the preparation of S-GQDs, the precursors were 1.68 g CA, 0.36 g Na2S·9H2O and 0.96 g NaOH, respectively, while for the S,N-GQDs, the starting materials were 1.68 g CA and 1.84 g Us in 40 mL DMF. The hydrothermal reactions of S-GQDs and S,N-GQDs both were performed at 180 °C for 8 h, referred to as S-GQD-8 and N,S-GQD-8. As the counterpart, the GQDs were produced under the same conditions as the processing of S-GQDs without the S precursor, recorded as GQD-8. All the synthesized GQDs were collected by adding ethanol into the solution and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and then ultrasonic cleaned with ethanol for 3 times.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope. The morphology of the N-GQD-8 was observed by atomic force microscope (AFM, Shimadzu SPM9700) operating at the semi-tapping mode. Raman scattering spectra were obtained using Horiba Jobin Yvon HR800 spectrometer with 532 nm laser. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Lambda 25 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerKinElmer, USA). The photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was recorded using a Shimadzu RF-5301 Fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a ThermoFisher-250XI X-ray electron spectrometer with focused monochromatized Al Kα radiation. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were collected with Edinburgh FLSP920 steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer, and the excitation wavelength is 367 nm. The QY of the as-prepared GQDs were measured by using two references of R6G in ethyl alcohol and quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4, where the QY of the two references are 98% and 54%, respectively. The calculation equation is given below:
(1)
∅
s
=
∅
r
×
I
s
I
r
×
A
r
A
s
×
n
s
2
n
r
2
where ϕs, Is, As, and ns represent the QY, emission peak area, integrated absorption intensity and refractive index of GQDs solutions, respectively, while the corresponding parameters with the subscript “r” refer to the references.

The doped GQDs were diluted by KH2PO4-K2HPO4 solution (0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4) to obtain a starting matrix (1.2 μg mL−1). The fluorescence intensity of the GQDs solution before (F0) and after (F) addition of DA were measured at 442 nm after excited at 350 nm. The exciting and emission slits were 3 and 5 nm, respectively. The mixed solution of GQDs and DA should be restored for 40 min before determining the fluorescence intensity in order to reach a stable adsorption. The detecting experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performances of the doped GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The insets in Fig. 1a give the pictures of four types of GQDs under visible and UV light irradiations, where all the GQDs emit blue fluorescence light. The UV–vis absorption spectra of S,N-GQDs and N-GQDs show two absorption bands (Fig. 1a), where the absorption peak at 240–270 nm can be attributed to the π → π* transition of C˭C domain [24–26], and the strongest absorption peak at 350 nm is related to the n → π* transition due to the nitrogen doping [27,28]. The PL emission wavelength of the blank GQD-8 and N-GQD-8 are at 442 nm, while these of S-GQD-8 and S,N-GQD-8 display a slight red shift of 2 and 7 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1b. Compared the full width half maximum (FWHM) of different GQDs (inset in Fig. 1b), N-GQD-8 has a narrower FWHM of 73 nm, indicating relatively uniform size distribution. After hydrothermal reaction, the QY of N-GQD-8 displays the largest value of 78% (to R6G), and then the S,N-GQD-8 (32%), S-GQD-8 (28%) and final the blank GQD-8 (14%) based on R6G as reference, as given in Fig. 1c. Therefore, the element doping can significantly improve the QY of GQDs, and N element shows the better doping effect. The fluorescence quenching performance of all types of GQDs toward DA are given in Fig. 1d. After addition of 20 μM DA, the N-GQD-8 exhibits the fastest quenching rate, and then the blank GQD-8, while the S,N-GQD-8 has very weak quenching performance, and S-GQD-8 almost has no quenching effect. Therefore, the GQDs after N doping display a stronger quenching effect to DA. In the following works, we focus on the effect of N doping on the quenching mechanism of GQDs to DA.

In order to further explore the effect of N doping on fluorescence performance, the detailed microstructure of the N-GQDs are investigated. Fig. 2a show the UV–vis and PL spectra of N-GQDs with different hydrothermal reaction duration and precursors. All the N-GQDs exhibit an obvious π → π* transition of C˭C at 240 nm and n → π* transition of C-N & C˭N conjugated structure at 350 nm [27]. The PL spectra of all the N-GQDs exhibit the same peak position at 442 nm. More interesting, excitation-independent emission of N-GQDs are observed. The N-GQD-8 is selected as an example to reflect the stable emission wavelength at 442 nm as the excitation wavelength varies from 350 to 400 nm (inset in Fig. 2a). Raman spectra of all the N-GQDs show an obvious disordered (D) and a crystalline (G) bands at 1352 and 1573 cm−1 (Fig. 2b), respectively [29,30]. The fitting data of N-GQD-8 shows the intensity ratio (ID/IG) is 0.8, which is lower than that of the common graphene material (approx.1.05) [29], indicating a relatively high crystallinity structure induced by N doping.

The QY of the N-GQDs with Ur as N source increases with reaction duration, reaches a maximum at 8 h (76% to quinine sulfate and 78% to R6G), and that of the N-GQD-16 is close to N-GQD-8, as given in Fig. 2c. Furthermore, the N-GQD-EDA-4 displays the highest QY (95% to R6G) among all the samples. However, the N-GQD-EDA-4 shows the worst quenching performance toward DA (Fig. 2d) compared with the other N-GQDs with Ur as N source, i.e. N-GQD-4, N-GQD-8, and N-GQD-16. The abnormal result implies that there are other intrinsic mechanisms to control the quenching performance of GQDs.

The N-GQD-8 is optimal due to the better quenching efficiency and QY. The detailed microstructure of this specimen is shown in Fig. 3. The as-prepared N-GQD-8 exhibit spherical or nearly spherical particles, which homogeneously distribute in the solution, as observed from the TEM image (Fig. 3a). The statistic results from 5 pieces of TEM images indicate that particle sizes range from 1 to 6 nm with an average size of 4 nm (inset in Fig. 3a). High-resolution (HRTEM) image and the inverse Fourier transform pattern (inset in Fig. 3b) clearly displays the hexagonal graphene network with lattice fringes of 0.25 and 0.21 nm (Fig. 3b). The observed results are fit well with the (11
2
̅
0) and (01
1
̅
0) planes of graphene [31,32], as illustrated in Fig. 3c. The AFM image of N-GQD-8 also gives the morphology of the granular particles (Fig. 3d), and the topographic heights of the particles are in the range of 0.6–3.0 nm corresponding to the thickness of 1–9 graphene layers (inset in Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection sensitivity of the N-GQDs to DA
ce:section.ce:para:
The concentration-dependent fluorescence response is shown in Fig. 4a. Surprisingly, the quenching intensity (y = (F0-F)/F0) of N-GQD-8 exhibits a dual linear relationship with the concentration of DA in the range of 10–3000 nM and 3000–7000 nM (Fig. 4b). The regression equations are:
y
1
= 1.6 ×
10
− 3
+ 1.2 ×
10
− 4
C
(nM) and
y
2
= 0.18 + 6.0 ×
10
− 5
C
(nM), and the coefficients of determination (R2) are 0.9988 and 0.9939 fitted from the least-squares method, respectively. The LOD of the two equations are estimated to be 3.3 and 611 nM (3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation for the blank solution, n = 10, and S is the slope of the calibration curve). The LOD of the first-order linear relationship is much lower than the reported values previously [19,20,24,33–36], as listed in Table 1. The dual linear relationship is unusual, but still has been reported in some fluorescence sensing methods, such as N-doped carbon dots for detecting Fe3+ in acidic water [3], S-doped GQDs for detecting Hg2+ [13], which both exhibit a dual linear relationship of fluorescence intensity with concentration. Although the researchers do not explain the origin to generate this phenomenon, we discuss the reasons in Section 4.2.

When determining the biomolecules in the biological media, not only the sensitivity but also the selectivity should be considered because of the complexity of human blood system [14,19–22]. In order to investigate the selectivity of the fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs toward DA in blood, common interference species with the physiological levels of 10 μM are considered, such as UA, AA, Ur, Gs, L-Cys, Gly and β-PEA. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, N-GQD-8 can be quenched only by the addition of DA, and the other interferences only result in 0.3–5% relative errors in the fluorescence intensity, therefore, exhibit a negligible effect on the determination of DA.

To demonstrate the practical feasibility of the nanosensor, DA detection in human serum samples was carried out by using the standard addition method. The serum sample was diluted 100-fold, and the results obtained are shown in Table 2. The recoveries of DA in the real samples are between 98.4% and 105.8%. Thus, the proposed method may hold potential application for DA detection in biological samples with a quick and simple manner.

In this work, we focus on the effects of element doping and QY on the fluorescence quenching of GQDs to DA. Two interesting works are found, one is that the N doping is more beneficial than the S doping for the fluorescence quenching of GQDs, and further raises the sensitivity to detect the DA (LOD = 3.3 nM). The other is that the high QY is not necessary to produce high sensitivity, as confirmed by the N-GQD-EDA-4 with QY up to 95% with no quenching performance. Thus, there are two problems left, why the N doping generates a high sensitivity, and what is the key structure or quenching mechanism to control the sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the fine structures on the quenching performance
ce:section.ce:para: Because the doped GQDs display similar sizes and size distributions, the surface chemical groups are thought to be crucial. The XPS survey spectra of the N-GQD-8 and N-GQD-EDA-4 show a predominant N 1s peak at 399.6 eV compared with the blank GQD-8 ( Fig. 5a). The atomic ratio of N/C in N-GQD-EDA-4 is calculated to be 39.2%, which is higher than that of the N-GQD-8 (22.8%). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of the N-GQD-8 (Fig. 5b) reveals the formation of pyridinic (399.3 eV), pyrrolic (400.3 eV) and graphitic N atoms (401.6 eV) [37,38]. However, deconvolution of N 1s spectrum of the N-GQD-EDA-4 only exhibits the pyrrolic (400.3 eV) and graphitic N atoms (401.6 eV), as shown in Fig. 5c. The XPS results of S-GQD-8, and N,S-GQD-8 are provided in Supplementary Fig. S1. The total N content of all the GQDs containing N element determined by XPS are given in Fig. 5d, where the N content in descending order is 16.8%, 14.8%, 11.3%, 11.1%, and 8.0% of the N-GQD-EDA-4, N,S-GQD-8, N-GQD-16, N-GQD-8, and N-GQD-4, respectively (see Supplementary Fig. S2). The N-GQD-EDA-4 has the highest N content of 16.8%, because the precursor of the EDA has two active groups of primary amine, which can stimulate the cyclization reaction between the citric acid and EDA by dehydration, as reported in Ref. [39]. The total N content of the N-GQD-8 and N-GQD-16 are 11.3% and 11.1%, respectively, which are higher than the 8.0% of N-GQD-4, suggesting that the total N content increases with increasing reaction time, and then reaches a saturation level after 16 h in the hydrothermal reaction. Combined with Figs. 1c and 2c, the higher N content leads to a higher QY except the S,N-GQD sample, which might result from the S doping.

According to the XPS results, two findings should be pointed out: (1) The N-GQD-EDA-4 has the highest total N content and contains no pyridinic N structure, and this specimen exhibits no quenching performance to DA (see Fig. 2d). (2) All the specimens of N-GDQ-4, N-GDQ-8 and N-GDQ-16 have pyridinic N structure, and display an obviously quenching performance, where the quenching rate increases with increasing pyridinic N content and reaches a saturation at the pyridinic N content up to 4.0%. Therefore, we speculate that the pyridinic N is the critical structure to promote fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs to DA. Since the pyridinic N atoms can provide an electron pair to the π system across the C atoms and form delocalized electrons [40–42], which can accelerate the formation of conjugated structure between GQDs and DAs, resulting in a strong quenching effect. That is the origin of the one-order linear relationship with high sensitivity, and the detailed explanations are given in Section 4.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of N-GQDs to DA
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching can be separated into static and dynamic quenching according to the degree of bimodular quenching constant (Kq) [1,3]. The Kq can be calculated by the equations as follows:
(2)
K
q
=
K
SV
τ
0
where τ0 is fluorescence lifetime. The KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, which is explored by:
(3)
F
0
F
=
1 +
K
SV
∙ [
C
]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher, and C is the concentration of the quencher. The plot of (F0/F) against C shows a linear range from 10 to 3000 nM with a KSV of 1.08
×
105 M−1. The τ0 of N-GQD-8 is 4.92 ns, and the estimated Kq is 2.2
×
1013 M−1 s−1. In diffusion-controlled quenching, the Kq is near 1010 M−1 s−1 for dynamic quenching, while that is several orders larger than 1010 M−1 s−1 for static quenching [1]. Therefore, the DA-quenched N-GQDs belong to the static quenching, which means that ground-state non-fluorescent complexes are formed between fluorescent molecules and quenchers.

In order to investigate the complex state between N-GQDs and DA, we further explore the fluorescence quenching behavior of N-GQD-8 to the DA homologues (tyramine, β-phenethylamine, catechol), which have highly similar microstructure to DA molecule ( Fig. 6a). Fig. 6b shows the PL intensity versus quenching time after addition of DA homologues. It is interesting to find that the β-phenethylamine and tyramine have no fluorescence quenching phenomenon, while the catechol and DA both exhibit fluorescence quenching. The catechol and DA biomolecules have the common chemical structure of catechol group (see Fig. 6a), thus, we deduce that the catechol group is the unique structure for the fluorescence quenching of the N-GQDs. Furthermore, the catechol displays faster quenching rate than the DA, but the final fluorescence intensity is close to that of DA. The reason for this can be attributed to the steric hindrance of the β-ethylamine group in the DA, which retards the adsorption of DA on the surface of N-GQDs. When the adsorption reaches a balance with the extension of staying time, the final fluorescence intensity of the DA is very close to that of the catechol, as shown in Fig. 6c and the inset. Commonly, the catechol group is susceptible to be oxidized into quinone, which might also influence the fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs [42]. However, the o-benzoquinone and DA-quinone obtained by the oxidation of catechol and DA by H2O2 exhibit no quenching effect to N-GQDs (Fig. 6d and the inset), which further prove that the formation of complex between catechol group and the N-GQD is the intrinsic quenching mechanism.

As we discussed above, the fluorescence quenching performance of N-GQD toward DA is strongly related to the pyridinic N structure in N-GQDs and the catechol group in DA. The detailed quenching mechanism of N-GQD to DA is illustrated in Fig. 7. N-GQDs emit blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. DA molecules exhibit co-existing balance with phenoxy anions and H+ in PBS solution, where the phenoxy anions are susceptible to adsorb on the surface of N-GQDs, preferentially the pyridinic N sites, due to the specific donor-bridge-acceptor structures with a large conjugated domain. The large delocalized π conjugating structure links the GQDs and DA molecules to form a ground-state non-fluorescent complex, as shown in the center of Fig. 7. This complex structure excludes the other interferences, resulting in a better selectivity. Due to the anchoring effect of pyridinic N in N-GQDs, the sensitivity is enhanced by the strong electron-donating groups [43–45]. The quenching of N-GQDs by DA is confined in one GQD particle with many DA molecules, estimated from the particle size and layer thickness of N-GQD-8 according to the TEM and AFM results. This stage is the first-order linear relationship for the detection of DA with concentration lower than 3000 nM. However, with increasing concentration of DA, self-polymerization of DAs might occur to form pDAs [19–22,44], resulting in a relatively lower sensitivity (LOD = 611 nM). Eventually, the cross-linking of the N-GQDs complexing with DA molecules is triggered. Accompanied with the aggregation of N-GQDs/pDAs, spheroidizing occurs gradually, and the particle size of the complexes is about 50 nm in the DA solution of 7000 nM, as observed from the TEM images. That is the second-order linear relationship. Briefly, the one-order linear relationship is originated from the formation of complex compounds between the pyridinic N in N-GQDs and the catechol group in DAs, resulting in a better sensitivity and selectivity. The two-order linear relationship is caused by the cross-linking between the particles of the N-GQD/DA due to the self-polymerization of DA molecules to form aggregates at higher concentration.

In this work, we investigate the doping effect of N and S elements on the fluorescence quenching performance of GQDs to DA. The N and S doped GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal reaction with precursors of CA as C source, Ur and EDA as N source, and Us as N and S co-doped materials. The element doping significantly enhances the QY of the GQDs, where the QY is 14% of GQD, 28% of S-GQD, 32% of S,N-GQD, 78% of N-GQD-8 and 95% of N-GQD-EDA-4. The N-GQD-8 exhibits an excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward the detection of DA, which shows a dual linear relationship with a wide concentration range of 10–7000 nM. The LOD of N-GQD-8 is 3.3 nM in 0.1 M PBS solution, which satisfies the regulatory requirement. The high sensitivity of the N-GQD-8 originates from a decisive pyridinic N structure, which accelerates the selective adsorption of catechol group in DA molecules on the surface of N-GQDs and forms a ground-state non-fluorescent complex between the N-GQDs and DA. The proposed sensing method is simple and low-cost with facile experimental operations, and it has a high selectivity for DA detection over a number of possible interfering species.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants 51771121, 11572281, 51572173 and 51602197). Prof. Wang gratefully acknowledges the fund provided by International Cooperation Project of Shanghai Municipal Science and Technology Commission (15520720300, 16060502300, 16JC402200 and 18511110600) and Shanghai Eastern Scholar Program (QD2016014). The financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (grant 2017YFA0204403) are also gratefully acknowledged by authors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2019.01.001.
